LSTM-Based Next Word Prediction¶

From Raw Text to Intelligent Word Generation¶

¶

Project Overview¶

This project demonstrates the complete implementation of an LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) based Next Word Prediction model developed from scratch. Rather than only training a neural network, the notebook explains every mathematical and preprocessing step involved in transforming raw text into meaningful predictions.

The objective is to illustrate the entire NLP pipeline—from tokenization and sequence generation to embedding, LSTM learning, dense layer computation, probability estimation, and final word prediction.

Methodology¶

The complete workflow is illustrated in the methodology diagram above.

1. Text Preprocessing¶

  • Input raw text corpus
  • Extract sentences
  • Generate a vocabulary
  • Assign a unique integer token to every word

2. Sequence Generation¶

  • Convert each sentence into numerical token sequences
  • Create multiple input-output training pairs
  • Apply zero-padding to produce fixed-length input vectors

3. Label Encoding¶

  • Convert output words into One-Hot Encoded vectors for supervised learning.

4. Embedding Layer¶

  • Transform sparse token IDs into dense continuous vector representations.
  • Learn semantic relationships between words during training.

5. LSTM Network¶

  • Process embedded sequences through LSTM cells.
  • Capture contextual and long-term dependencies between words.

6. Dense Layer¶

  • Perform matrix multiplication

[ \mathbf{z}= \mathbf{h}W+b ]

where

  • (h) = LSTM hidden state
  • (W) = Dense layer weights
  • (b) = Bias vector

7. Softmax Prediction¶

Convert logits into probabilities

[ P(y_i)=\frac{e^{z_i}}{\sum_j e^{z_j}} ]

The token with the highest probability becomes the predicted next word.

Pipeline Summary¶

Raw Text
    │
    ▼
Tokenization
    │
    ▼
Sequence Generation
    │
    ▼
Padding
    │
    ▼
One-Hot Encoding
    │
    ▼
Embedding Layer
    │
    ▼
LSTM
    │
    ▼
Dense Layer
    │
    ▼
Softmax
    │
    ▼
Next Word Prediction

Key Learning Objectives¶

✔ Understand NLP preprocessing from first principles

✔ Visualize how tokenization creates numerical datasets

✔ Learn how embedding converts integers into dense vectors

✔ Understand LSTM memory flow

✔ Follow Dense layer computations mathematically

✔ Interpret Softmax probabilities for next-word prediction

🛠 Technologies Used¶

  • Python
  • NumPy
  • TensorFlow / Keras
  • LSTM
  • Embedding Layer
  • One-Hot Encoding
  • Jupyter Notebook

Introduction to Long Short Term Memory
Last Updated : 18 May, 2026

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is an improved version of the Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) designed to capture long-term dependencies in sequential data. It uses a memory cell to store information over time, solving the limitations of traditional RNNs.

    Handles Long Term Dependencies: Remembers information for longer sequences
    Memory Cell: Stores and updates important information over time
    Better than RNN: Overcomes short term memory limitations
    Applications: Used in language translation, speech recognition and time series forecasting

introduction_to_lstms.webpintroduction_to_lstms.webp
Problem with Long-Term Dependencies in RNN

RNNs are designed to handle sequential data by using a hidden state that stores information from previous steps. However, they struggle to learn long-term dependencies. This happens due to:

    Vanishing Gradient: When training a model over time, the gradients which help the model learn can shrink as they pass through many steps. This makes it hard for the model to learn long-term patterns since earlier information becomes almost irrelevant.
    Exploding Gradient: Sometimes gradients can grow too large causing instability. This makes it difficult for the model to learn properly as the updates to the model become erratic and unpredictable.

LSTM Architecture

LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) architecture is designed to learn long-term dependencies in sequential data using memory cells and gates that control the flow of information through the network.
Main Gates in LSTM

    Input Gate: Decides which new information should be added to the memory cell
    Forget Gate: Determines which information should be removed from the memory cell
    Output Gate: Controls which information from the memory cell is passed to the next hidden state and output

Working of LSTM

LSTM consists of a repeating chain like structure with memory cells and gating mechanisms
gate_of_lstm
LSTM Model

Information is retained by the cells and the memory manipulations are done by thegates. There are three gates:
1. Forget Gate

The forget gate decides which information should be kept or removed from the cell state. It uses the current input x_t and previous hidden state h_{t-1} then applies a sigmoid function to generate values between 0 and 1.

    Values close to 0 remove information
    Values close to 1 retain information
    Helps discard unnecessary past information
    Controls memory retention in the LSTM

The equation for the forget gate is:

    f_t = \sigma \left( W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f \right)

Where

    W_f represents the weight matrix associated with the forget gate.
    [h_t-1, x_t] denotes the concatenation of the current input and the previous hidden state.
    b_f is the bias with the forget gate.
    \sigma is the sigmoid activation function.

forget_gate
Forget Gate
2. Input gate

The addition of useful information to the cell state is done by the input gate.

    First the information is regulated using the sigmoid function and filter the values to be remembered similar to the forget gate using inputs h_{t-1} and x_t.
    Then, a vector is created using tanh function that gives an output from -1 to +1 which contains all the possible values from h_{t-1} and x_t.
    At last the values of the vector and the regulated values are multiplied to obtain the useful information. 

The equation for the input gate is:

    i_t = \sigma \left( W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i \right)

    \hat{C}_t = \tanh \left( W_c \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_c \right)

We multiply the previous state by f_t effectively filtering out the information we had decided to ignore earlier. Then we add i_t \odot C_t which represents the new candidate values scaled by how much we decided to update each state value.

    C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \hat{C}_t

where

    \odot denotes element-wise multiplication
    tanh is activation function

input_gate
Input Gate
3. Output gate

The output gate determines which information from the current cell state should be passed as the hidden state (output) at the current time step. It uses the previous hidden state h_{t - 1}​ and the current input x_t​ ​, followed by a sigmoid function to control the output flow.

    o_t = \sigma \left( W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o \right)

Next, the current cell state C_t​ is passed through a tanh activation to scale its values between -1 and +1. Finally, this transformed cell state is multiplied element-wise with o_t​ to produce the hidden state h_t:

    h_t = o_t \odot \tanh(C_t)

Here:

    o_t​ is the output gate activation.
    C_t​ is the current cell state.
    \odot represents element-wise multiplication.
    \sigma is the sigmoid activation function.

This hidden state h_t ​ is then passed to the next time step and can also be used for generating the output of the network.
output_gate
Output Gate
Applications

    Language modeling for machine translation and text summarization
    Speech recognition for converting audio into text
    Time series forecasting for stock prices, weather and energy usage
    Anomaly detection for fraud and intrusion detection
    Recommender systems for personalized suggestions
    Video analysis for activity recognition and motion understanding

Understanding of LSTM Networks
Visit Coursecourse link icon
Suggested Quiz

4 Questions

What is the main purpose of using LSTM networks over traditional RNNs?

    A

    LSTMs require fewer computations during training
    B

    LSTMs retain information across longer sequences
    C

    LSTMs use a simpler network architecture
    D

    LSTMs are designed mainly for image analysis


success

Comment

A
aakarshachug

Why Do We Need Tokenization?¶

Deep learning models such as LSTM cannot process raw text directly. They can only work with numerical values. Therefore, we must convert text into numbers before feeding it to the model.

Example¶

Input sentence:

"My name is Srabon"

After Tokenization:

Word Token ID
My 1
name 2
is 3
Srabon 4

Tokenized sequence:

[1, 2, 3, 4]
In [21]:
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.text import Tokenizer
In [22]:
tokenizer=Tokenizer()
In [23]:
tokenizer.fit_on_texts([text])   #  Multiple texts can be passed in the form of a list. Here we have passed only one text.
In [24]:
tokenizer.word_index
Out[24]:

Avobe we see how the text assigned to numbers¶

  • Now Create dataset like supervised learnign:
  • Let see a small example:

"My name is Srabon"

After Tokenization:

Word Token ID
My 1
name 2
is 3
Srabon 4

Dataset Created

InputOutput
Myname
My name is
My name is Srabon

Tokenized Dataset

InputOutput
[1][2]
[1,2][3]
[1,2,3][4]

Introduction to Long Short Term Memory
Last Updated : 18 May, 2026

Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) is an improved version of the Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) designed to capture long-term dependencies in sequential data. It uses a memory cell to store information over time, solving the limitations of traditional RNNs.

    Handles Long Term Dependencies: Remembers information for longer sequences
    Memory Cell: Stores and updates important information over time
    Better than RNN: Overcomes short term memory limitations
    Applications: Used in language translation, speech recognition and time series forecasting

introduction_to_lstms.webpintroduction_to_lstms.webp
Problem with Long-Term Dependencies in RNN

RNNs are designed to handle sequential data by using a hidden state that stores information from previous steps. However, they struggle to learn long-term dependencies. This happens due to:

    Vanishing Gradient: When training a model over time, the gradients which help the model learn can shrink as they pass through many steps. This makes it hard for the model to learn long-term patterns since earlier information becomes almost irrelevant.
    Exploding Gradient: Sometimes gradients can grow too large causing instability. This makes it difficult for the model to learn properly as the updates to the model become erratic and unpredictable.

LSTM Architecture

LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) architecture is designed to learn long-term dependencies in sequential data using memory cells and gates that control the flow of information through the network.
Main Gates in LSTM

    Input Gate: Decides which new information should be added to the memory cell
    Forget Gate: Determines which information should be removed from the memory cell
    Output Gate: Controls which information from the memory cell is passed to the next hidden state and output

Working of LSTM

LSTM consists of a repeating chain like structure with memory cells and gating mechanisms
gate_of_lstm
LSTM Model

Information is retained by the cells and the memory manipulations are done by thegates. There are three gates:
1. Forget Gate

The forget gate decides which information should be kept or removed from the cell state. It uses the current input x_t and previous hidden state h_{t-1} then applies a sigmoid function to generate values between 0 and 1.

    Values close to 0 remove information
    Values close to 1 retain information
    Helps discard unnecessary past information
    Controls memory retention in the LSTM

The equation for the forget gate is:

ight)_t = \sigma \left( W_f \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_f 

Where

    W_f represents the weight matrix associated with the forget gate.
    [h_t-1, x_t] denotes the concatenation of the current input and the previous hidden state.
    b_f is the bias with the forget gate.
    \sigma is the sigmoid activation function.

forget_gate
Forget Gate
2. Input gate

The addition of useful information to the cell state is done by the input gate.

    First the information is regulated using the sigmoid function and filter the values to be remembered similar to the forget gate using inputs h_{t-1} and x_t.
    Then, a vector is created using tanh function that gives an output from -1 to +1 which contains all the possible values from h_{t-1} and x_t.
    At last the values of the vector and the regulated values are multiplied to obtain the useful information. 

The equation for the input gate is:

ight)_t = \sigma \left( W_i \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_i 

ight)hat{C}_t = 	anh \left( W_c \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_c 

We multiply the previous state by f_t effectively filtering out the information we had decided to ignore earlier. Then we add i_t \odot C_t which represents the new candidate values scaled by how much we decided to update each state value.

    C_t = f_t \odot C_{t-1} + i_t \odot \hat{C}_t

where

    \odot denotes element-wise multiplication
    tanh is activation function

input_gate
Input Gate
3. Output gate

The output gate determines which information from the current cell state should be passed as the hidden state (output) at the current time step. It uses the previous hidden state h_{t - 1}​ and the current input x_t​ ​, followed by a sigmoid function to control the output flow.

ight)_t = \sigma \left( W_o \cdot [h_{t-1}, x_t] + b_o 

Next, the current cell state C_t​ is passed through a tanh activation to scale its values between -1 and +1. Finally, this transformed cell state is multiplied element-wise with o_t​ to produce the hidden state h_t:

    h_t = o_t \odot 	anh(C_t)

Here:

    o_t​ is the output gate activation.
    C_t​ is the current cell state.
    \odot represents element-wise multiplication.
    \sigma is the sigmoid activation function.

This hidden state h_t ​ is then passed to the next time step and can also be used for generating the output of the network.
output_gate
Output Gate
Applications

    Language modeling for machine translation and text summarization
    Speech recognition for converting audio into text
    Time series forecasting for stock prices, weather and energy usage
    Anomaly detection for fraud and intrusion detection
    Recommender systems for personalized suggestions
    Video analysis for activity recognition and motion understanding

Understanding of LSTM Networks
Visit Coursecourse link icon
Suggested Quiz

4 Questions

What is the main purpose of using LSTM networks over traditional RNNs?

    A

    LSTMs require fewer computations during training
    B

    LSTMs retain information across longer sequences
    C

    LSTMs use a simpler network architecture
    D

    LSTMs are designed mainly for image analysis


success

Login to View Explanation

1/4
Comment

A
aakarshachug

Now we make out dataset use those vectors using loop :¶

In [28]:
input_sequences = []
for sentense in text.split("\n"):
    token_vector=tokenizer.texts_to_sequences([sentense])[0]
    for i in range(1, len(token_vector)):
        n_gram_sequence=token_vector[:i+1]
        input_sequences.append(n_gram_sequence)  # Adding the n-gram sequence to the list of input sequences. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".
In [29]:
for i in input_sequences:
    print(i)  # Printing the n-gram sequences generated from the text. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".

[83, 443, 444, 445, 446, 159, 447, 448]

This is input and output column ( last number is uoutput rest is input)¶

  • A jaint List n**2
  • n is number of word in each sentence

This is not perfect for LSTM

  • why? -> Each sentence have defferent in size But in LSTM takes Same size vector
  • so we resize then and make same dimentional vector
    table>

Tokenized Dataset

InputOutput
Myname
My name is
My name is Srabon

Resized Tokenized Dataset

Input(extra spece must be fill by 0 prefix ) Output
[0,0,1][2]
[0,1,2][3]
[1,2,3][4]
In [30]:
mx=0
for i in input_sequences:
    mx=max(mx, len(i))  # Finding the maximum length of the n-gram sequences generated from the text. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".
In [31]:
mx
Out[31]:
51
In [32]:
from tensorflow.keras.preprocessing.sequence import pad_sequences
padded_seq=pad_sequences(input_sequences, maxlen=mx, padding='pre')    # Sutometically pads the sequences to the maximum length found in the input_sequences list, using pre-padding (adding zeros at the beginning of the sequences).

Now Separate Input and Output¶

  • Input -> 0 to len-2
  • Output-> Last element of the List
In [33]:
X=padded_seq[:,:-1 ]  # all without the last sequence, which will be used as the target for training the model. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".
In [34]:
print (X)  # Printing the input sequences after padding. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".

[[  0   0   0 ...   0   0  64]
 [  0   0   0 ...   0   0 192]
 [  0   0   0 ...   0   0  23]
 ...
 [  0   0   0 ... 444 445 446]
 [  0   0   0 ... 445 446 159]
 [  0   0   0 ... 446 159 447]]
In [35]:
Output=padded_seq[:, -1]  # last sequence will be used as the target for training the model. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".
In [36]:
Output  # Printing the output sequences after padding. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".
Out[36]:
array([191, 193,  48, ..., 159, 447, 448], shape=(1056,), dtype=int32)

Now it has some Problem if we consider it as a reggesion because the output is descreate number

  • output = [ 191, 193,48,......]
  • So we use one hot encodding for classify them which make the output sequence in such a way we classify the output
  • Lets understand:

Resized Tokenized Dataset

Input(extra spece must be fill by 0 prefix ) Output
[0,0,1][2]
[0,1,2][3]
[1,2,3][4]

This table transform into :

   Resized Tokenized Dataset(One hot encodded)
Input(extra spece must be fill by 0 prefix ) Output( One hot encodded for classify)
[0,0,1][0,1,0,0] ( 2)
[0,1,2][0,0,1,0](3)
[1,2,3][0,0,0,1](4)
In [37]:
import numpy as np
import tensorflow as tf
from tensorflow.keras.utils import to_categorical
Output=to_categorical(Output, num_classes=len(tokenizer.word_index)+1)  # Converting the output sequences to one-hot encoded format. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".
In [38]:
X
Out[38]:
array([[  0,   0,   0, ...,   0,   0,  64],
       [  0,   0,   0, ...,   0,   0, 192],
       [  0,   0,   0, ...,   0,   0,  23],
       ...,
       [  0,   0,   0, ..., 444, 445, 446],
       [  0,   0,   0, ..., 445, 446, 159],
       [  0,   0,   0, ..., 446, 159, 447]], shape=(1056, 50), dtype=int32)

Input and Output creation done perfectly now we go to LSTM architecture

Core Conceptual Architecture of LSTM¶

In [39]:
from tensorflow.keras.models import Sequential
from tensorflow.keras.layers import Input, Embedding, LSTM, Dense

model = Sequential([
    Input(shape=(50,)),          # Only sequence length
    Embedding(input_dim=449, output_dim=100),
    LSTM(150),
    Dense(449, activation="softmax")
])

model.summary()

E0000 00:00:1783971966.113839  482596 cuda_platform.cc:52] failed call to cuInit: INTERNAL: CUDA error: Failed call to cuInit: UNKNOWN ERROR (303)

Model: "sequential"

┏━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┳━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┓
┃ Layer (type)                    ┃ Output Shape           ┃       Param # ┃
┡━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━╇━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━┩
│ embedding (Embedding)           │ (None, 50, 100)        │        44,900 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ lstm (LSTM)                     │ (None, 150)            │       150,600 │
├─────────────────────────────────┼────────────────────────┼───────────────┤
│ dense (Dense)                   │ (None, 449)            │        67,799 │

[ \begin{aligned} \textbf{Input Tokens} &= \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1\\ 0 & 1 & 2\\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{bmatrix} \\[12pt] \textbf{Embedding Matrix }E &= \begin{bmatrix} 0.10 & 0.20 & 0.30 & 0.40 & 0.50\\ 0.60 & 0.70 & 0.80 & 0.90 & 1.00\\ 1.10 & 1.20 & 1.30 & 1.40 & 1.50\\ 1.60 & 1.70 & 1.80 & 1.90 & 2.00 \end{bmatrix} \\[12pt] \textbf{Embedded Output} &= \begin{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.10&0.20&0.30&0.40&0.50\\ 0.10&0.20&0.30&0.40&0.50\\ 0.60&0.70&0.80&0.90&1.00 \end{bmatrix} \\[10pt] \begin{bmatrix} 0.10&0.20&0.30&0.40&0.50\\ 0.60&0.70&0.80&0.90&1.00\\ 1.10&1.20&1.30&1.40&1.50 \end{bmatrix} \\[10pt] \begin{bmatrix} 0.60&0.70&0.80&0.90&1.00\\ 1.10&1.20&1.30&1.40&1.50\\ 1.60&1.70&1.80&1.90&2.00 \end{bmatrix} \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned} ]

In [22]:
model.compile(optimizer='adam', loss='categorical_crossentropy',metrics=['accuracy'])  # Compiling the model with Adam optimizer and categorical cross-entropy loss function. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".
In [49]:
model.fit(X, Output_one_hot, epochs=100, batch_size=32)  # Training the model using the input sequences and one-hot encoded output sequences. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".

KeyboardInterrupt:
In [56]:
test_text="architecture"  

for i in range(10):
    token_text=tokenizer.texts_to_sequences([test_text])[0] 
    padded_token=pad_sequences([token_text], maxlen=50) 
    predicted=model.predict(padded_token) 
    ind= np.argmax(predicted)
    for word,index in tokenizer.word_index.items():
        if index==ind:
            test_text=test_text+" "+word
            print(test_text)  # Printing the generated text after predicting the next word using the trained model. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".

1/1 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 110ms/step
architecture is

architecture is retained

architecture is retained by
1/1 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 56ms/step
architecture is retained by the

architecture is retained by the cells

architecture is retained by the cells and

architecture is retained by the cells and the
1/1 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 104ms/step
architecture is retained by the cells and the memory
1/1 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 68ms/step
architecture is retained by the cells and the memory manipulations
1/1 ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 0s 63ms/step
architecture is retained by the cells and the memory manipulations are

At the last cell architecture find the next word and add it to the test_text variable. Then print the updated test_text variable. Each paragraph is separated by a new line character "\n".¶

This notebook is designed as an educational walkthrough that explains not only what happens inside an LSTM-based next-word prediction model, but also why each step is necessary.
